sentences.text: Yes.
First of all-- first of all, I dropped out from the Department of Agriculture on account of one professor, Lelesz, who was well known.
And, you know, he took revenge on me for that.
Then when-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] in 1936, '37, when they restarted all the troubles in university-- and they made a what's called academic ghetto, the Jews had to sit on the left side, and so on.
I was already, at that time, assistant, you know.
I, I-- 1936, in two years, I got scholarships from the university after I returned, which permitted me to drop most of the-- my tutoring and some other obligations.
And so I-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] ones and twos passed all the exams, except one.
The most stupid exam was a needed exam-- science exam.
One professor, who kept me all the time-- cutting me down, cutting keep me down.
And finally, I came, and I was, was complaining, complaining to my professor.
I say, on this account-- on account of Professor [NON-ENGLISH], I wouldn't be able to, to graduate ever and ever.
Because three times-- we were permitted to take the same exam from this same subject three times.
And three times, his paleontology, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] he flunked me.
So of course, Professor Limanowski, my main professor, and Professor [NON-ENGLISH], my supporter, and [NON-ENGLISH], another, and [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH]-- they went to him.
And later, I learned that he did it because Lelesz asked him.
But anyway, he gave me a C on the condition that some other grades will be cut by 10%.
And you say the Jewish students already, at that point, were separated from the--
Not-- wasn't--
--separated.
It was '36.
So I graduated with, with honor.
I, I asked my-- and I decided to make a doctorate.

And I asked my professor to accept me as a assistant in department, which was quite unusual that-- because geographers had a tacit understanding among themselves in Poland not to take minorities as assistants because of the trouble in Lemberg, in Lwow-- who was one of the Ukrainian Kubiyovych, Volodymyr Kubiyovych.
Brilliant man, really brilliant, maybe more brilliant than any one of young Polish geographers, because when he graduated, he got his PhD.
So he started to write against Poles and support-- we was-- he was Ukrainian, Ukrainian nationalism.
So since then, his professor, the famous-- world-famous Romer and others, made it-- no minorities on the National Chair of Geography.
And believe me, there were at least two brilliant-- probably more brilliant-- men than I was.
One was Zdislaw Simcher, who graduated Cracow.
And one was Josef Haliczer, who graduated [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Lwow or Lemberg.
And they never were accepted and never permitted to publish even in [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Polish geographical magazines.
They were two Jewish.
But I-- was one very, very good friend of mine.
Simcher perished during the Holocaust.
I don't know exactly how.
And Professor Haliczer, under an assumed name, survived.
And we met a couple times.
We knew each other before the war.
We met.
And I still have his books in our upstairs, in my study, whatever or not.
I was married in 1936, after I-- in April or May, I got my master's diploma.
And I got accepted as a assistant.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] At the very same time, the high school Tarbut, the Hebrew-- Hebrew high school, asked me to teach geography.
Because they tried to get so-called state rights.
In Poland, there were state schools and private schools.
Some private schools had what's called 'state rights.' It means during the final exams, they-- only their teachers were present.
And the others had usually a commission from Polish state schools.
And local school-- local teachers had little to say there.
And they were weak in geography and something else, you know.
And so he-- I accepted as a service.
Because essentially, I didn't need-- because-- although I asked to be a private docent-- private docent of the department.
And Professor Limanowski right away somehow found for me funds.
And I was paid as a regular assistant.
And not only paid, but within the three years, I advanced all the time-- a proper step.
So I, I had money.
I, I married Sulamite Staropolska--
--of Dr.-- of Dr.
Staropolski, who practiced in a town, Suwalki-- a very well-known Zionist in Poland.
He attended-- he was the first to work in the first three Congresses.
And the whole idea was that when she will graduate and I will have my PhD-- so we will go to Israel.
At that time already, I wrote to Professor [NON-ENGLISH] at Hebrew University.
And he advised me the better to make it-- a PhD either in Poland or in France.
And so, this is how we were ready to go.
And so I essentially had two jobs-- one the university, where I used to teach.
And there, I want to tell you a story.
When they introduced that left tables and benches for Jews and right for Poles-- so I conducted one of the classes.
So I came in.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And somebody from the class, a Pole, stood up and said, Magister Melezin, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there is a new, I don't know, regulation.
The Jews must be on the left side.
Therefore, the three students-- they pointed out my wife and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [NON-ENGLISH], another student, and the third-- should go on the other side.
So I stopped here [NON-ENGLISH].
Everybody sits the way they used to.
Who doesn't like it can walk out from the class.
Nobody walked out.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But then there were disturbances in Vilna.
And the students from agriculture-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] really, you know, biggest anti-Semites.
When I worked in a prosectorium, they used to throw us pieces of dead animals, you know, and so on.
So there was one girl I remember.
She was-- I think that she was of Tatar extraction.
So they blocked me in.
They came to the department, was a big-- throw out the Jew!
And we'll make an order with him.
So it wasn't so easy to make an order with me.
First of all, my janitor, Antony, told them the story-- a true story.
But he embellished it so much.
So they kept me, you know, in the-- my office until about 8:00 in the evening or 9:00 in the evening.
But I had a telephone.
So I could communicate with home.
I, I communicated with Professor Rewenska.
It was two professors actually I communicated that time.
They say, sit quiet.
And I was there.
Antony essentially said, look, he corrected a couple-- a couple heads during a trip to Turkey.
And he will correct your heads, too.
Oh, I had a son.
He was born [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] January 7, 1941.
All right.
Here starts a very interesting chain of incidents.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] When Germans attacked Poland, they occupied the city of [NON-ENGLISH].
And my in-laws fled to Lida.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] At that time, I returned from an-- I was supposed to organize a school for [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] officers and teach them geography, reading maps.
But, of course, nothing's as rapid.
When I returned, so-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I couldn't live in the place where I lived before.
So I lived temporarily with her cousin-- with my wife's cousin.
And then one day came my mother-in-law, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] who turned out to some kind of relative of Rachela's father.
Because their-- my mother-in-law, by the name-- maiden name [NON-ENGLISH] came from Lida.
Rachela's father, Pupko, was from Lida.
And so he-- so he-- she had one address, their address.
But she didn't know that her father died.
But she knew her mother, Malka Pupko.
She came to them.
And she lived at that time with her son Zelig and his wife, Dora, and two children, Osia and Abrasha.
And they had a large apartment.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So she asked whether she knows where she can arrange an apartment for us.
So it seems to me Dora at that time said, look, they already-- the Soviets already took away one room.
And we have two others rooms.
And I will-- I gladly will sublet them.
Because otherwise, Soviets will come in.
Or who knows who they will do?
And, you know, it's a-- it's a matter of [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] financial investment.
I tried to build a house for ourselves near the university.
Because a new-- despite that I knew that I won't be in Poland too long, I thought, you know.
So I knew that, that-- build house near university is a good investment in terms of money.
But, of course, when it was finished outside, it had to be finished inside-- when the Soviets came.
So the best thing to not to admit that you're a, a house owner.
This, this the problem.
So we moved with a child.
And no, we moved in into the two rooms.
The child was born.
And then Rachela's-- Sarah's nanny recommended me her girlfriend from the same village, or whatever it is as the nanny for Zarek.
You see?
Zarek was the name of your son.
Yeah-- I name him after my father, Eliezer-- Lazar, Lazarek, Zarek, you see?
Well, you know, after that, I knew Rachela.
I wasn't acquainted with Rachela yet when she was a student.
But everybody knew Pupko, because she was a beautiful girl.
Hmm?
Rachela was the daughter of Malka Pupko and the sister of Zelig Pupko.
And I moved in.
At that time, the Soviets disbanded the Hebrew school.
And they sent me to teach in the Yiddish school where Rachela was a teacher.
This was the year 1939.
Fall, 1939-- it, it means-- no, this was January 1940, exactly.
Because this way-- in the beginning, Soviets came, they emptied-- completely cleaned out the city from everything and then gave it back to Lithuania as their capital.
And they were there for-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Lithuanians came in October, it seems to me, to Vilnius.
And the Soviets moved out.
And the Hebrew school still operated.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And it operated also the next year in fall.
But at that time, I started to teach in another private Polish school, not yet in, in the Yiddish high school.
But then Soviets took over Lithuania.
They disbanded all Jewish life, practically-- Zionists in Zion-- and disbanded the school-- Tarbut school.
And they sent me to teach in Yiddish school and in the other school.
I got tremendous load.
I had a full load-- 42 hours in one school, an additional eight hours-- and that's 50, 50 hours.
And I had to go to make time, you know, to be on time, I had to-- almost to run from one school to another school because they were in different parts of, of the city.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] All right, so when Soviets-- and here, I want to tell you now example of incidents or accidents.
The Lithuanians were in Vilna.

So they had embassies of foreign powers-- and Japanese embassy and Swedish embassy, it seems to me; and it seems some Portuguese, too, you know-- used to give away visas-- transit visas-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] transit visas, you know, to-- and many of refugees in Vilna.
There was probably about 15,000 refugees in Vilna now from Poland.
And first, all the Zionists' hierarchy from all sorts, you know-- the Chalutz, and Center, and Betarim-- Betar, Jabotinsky people, you know-- so everybody tried to get the visa and get out.
So in December, 1939, I went to Lida.
So yeah-- so I told Sula, look, definitely I, as an academic man with doctorate degree and published work, and so on, I can get a visa either through Sweden or to Sweden or Japan east.
And then probably I would be able somehow to go south maybe-- I don't know, whether America-- I wouldn't know if America wouldn't let in, but I-- probably to Australia.
So she said to me, look, my parents are here in Lida.
Without them, I won't go.
So I went in December to Lida, found Dr.
Staropolski, present him the problems.
We can get visa.
You can get also visa, especially that there were money.
As I said, money open all the doors.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So he said, you know, he's a very big [INAUDIBLE] man.
He was supposed to be a rabbi.
They sent him to Berlin to study in the second yeshiva.
But in Berlin, he-- decided to be a doctor.
You know, he became more secular, although very learned man-- he spoke six or seven languages.
He said, look, my son, Isak, now is on the second year in university, Department of Medicine, Lemberg.
I'm not going to leave him alone.
And I can't bring him over.
You do whatever you want to.
I came, said this to my wife, Sula.
She says, I am not going.
So we didn't go.
Now, so I got stuck in Lithuania, with two-- in Vilnius.
At the time, Vilna became Vilnius.
And I had two jobs in two high schools.
Sula started to teach, also in-- hardly enough.
She started to teach in a school from which I was thrown out, which became now a, a Yiddish school.
But she used to teach it in Polish.
She used to teach biology.
She graduated biology.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] All right, so my-- from point of view of financial [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] situation, we were not bad off.
I threw out some of the furniture that I had in, in those two rooms when we rented it from Pupkos.
I bought some other furniture.
We had some rugs on the wall, and old kilims, and so on, in the back room where the child was, and other things, you know.
And of course, at that time, I remember I used to meet some old colleagues from university-- Dr.
Wanda Rewenska, the docent of the Department of Geography; Professor Limanowski; my wife-- Professor Demborski.
And at that time came that Ribbentrop Pact, Molotov-Ribbentrop Pact.
So I told him, look, you know, I said, this will be good for five years.
Because it will take-- in the best case, it will take five years-- literal take five years to make order in Western Europe.
So I was mistaken.
I was mistaken.
I never-- speaking frankly, I never-- you know, as a-- I was educated man, knew history.
I never expected that a leader of a nation who talked of a Reich for one thousand years or whatever or not, will make such a mistake to fight on two fronts.
And where?
In Russia.
So-- Did you hear what was happening in Germany at that time, with the Jews in--
We knew.
We knew.
My father-in-law didn't believe.
He said, look, these are rumors the way were rumors during World War I-- Germans not as bad people.
I graduated their university.
I have there many friends.
It is-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] so I said, look, take a look at what's going-- what's coming from Warsaw.
And at that time, Warsaw, they start to organize the ghetto, I said.
And-- but we knew that they killed off Jews in Posen.
He said, it is only rumors.
No.
I remember he said-- he said, Abrasha, aren't you ashamed that you university men believe in rumors without any proof?
You know?
And when did your situation begin to change in Vilna then, when you were--
Well, the situation--
--began to change when Germans came into Vilna.
In '41-- in June, '41, you know.
I remember it was Sunday.
And we went obligatory meeting of teachers in a big hall.
We went there 10 o'clock.
Around 12 o'clock, suddenly, we heard bombs coming from very far away.
And here comes out a commissar.
Comrades, we were attacked by a fascist-- surrounded our army.
You heroically fight, and whatever or not.
You go home.
And you'll get your orders, and whatever or not.
I, I came home about 11 o'clock-- o'clock, together with Sula.
Now this was my wife.
And by 12 o'clock, we saw German airplanes.
And they start to bombard a big building.
It was this-- In Polish tongue, it was the Center Administration of Railroads.
And the Soviets had there also administrative offices.
And this was just about one block away from our house when I lived-- when I rented those two rooms, you know.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And then the alarm sounded, you know.
We went all the way to the basement.
And, of course, I remember it now.
I took my child, and I covered myself this way.
And the bombardment stopped on the 24th.
On the second or third day of the war, Germans were there.
What happened-- you know, oh, I don't have to repeat all the thing, because there are books that describe it in detail what happened in Vilna.
Let me one thing that-- because I could not get certain things, especially milk, right there, you know, so I used to go all the way back to Snipiskes.
We went to that-- where we-- where my stepfather had the vegetable farm, you know.
And there I knew everybody.
And there I would get everything.
First of all, they started to catch people from the street.
People would disappear.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] One day, beginning of July, Rachela came.
They took away her husband, you know.
And they threw her out from her apartment.
But I have to tell you that Rachela married in a very wealthy family.
And her husband-- the family was one of richest in Vilna.
And everybody knew them.
And she was hated by Poles and Lithuanians, you know. 'Rich kikes' is what they'd called them, you know.
So Rachela had no place to go, so she came to live there.
And this how we became closer, you know.
By the way, let me tell you one thing.
When the Germans came, and I couldn't get maternity clothes-- not the Germans-- when, when the Soviets came.
So Rachela gave Sula, my wife, her maternity clothes.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then we became, you know, very friendly, you know, real friends, you know.
And anyway, so I used to go across the city, sometimes zigzagging.
But in one place, I couldn't zigzag.
This is when I crossed the River Vilnia.
And there one day, in the middle of July, on the corner of Kalvariska Street and Lwowska Street, I suddenly heard cries and shouts.
I turned to the left.
And I saw those Lithuanians-- we call them [NON-ENGLISH]; it means 'catchers,' you know-- drags a couple Jews.
So I decided to jump on the other side.
Because I had to make one block, and then I went through number 49.
From number 49, would go to 51, where was the farm of my uncle.
It means brother of my stepfather.
On the corner was a big house-- a two-, three-story house.
I remember, the owner was Baranowski.
And, you know, that house had such gates-- had gates.
And when I ran, ran on the other side, suddenly from the gate comes out a Lithuanian guy with a revolver.
Stop, you kike!
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
So I started, you know-- but he kept, you know-- he kept himself at a distance from me.
Either he knew what can happen to him with my physical, you know, because I was a very strong guy.
Rachela can testify that on the farm already, after the war, I could pick up with one hand two bushels of wheat and throw this way on the--
Yeah.
